content.content:
containers:
      - args:
        - --config.file=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml
        - --storage.tsdb.path=/prometheus
        - --web.enable-lifecycle
        image: prom/prometheus:v2.51.2
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        name: prometheus
        ports:
        - containerPort: 9090
          name: http
          protocol: TCP
        resources: {}
        terminationMessagePath: /dev/termination-log
        terminationMessagePolicy: File
        volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /etc/prometheus/
          name: config
        - mountPath: /prometheus
          name: data
      dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
      restartPolicy: Always
      schedulerName: default-scheduler
      securityContext: {}
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30
      volumes:
      - configMap:
          defaultMode: 420
          name: prometheus-config
        name: config
      - emptyDir: {}
        name: data

content.thinking:
Now I can see the issue clearly:

1. The Prometheus config only has a `prometheus` job scraping `localhost:9090` - it's not scraping RabbitMQ at all.
2. The RabbitMQ pods expose metrics on port 15692 at `/metrics`.
3. There's a headless service `rabbitmq-headless` that I can use to address individual pods by their DNS names.

The fix is to update the `prometheus-config` ConfigMap to add a scrape job for RabbitMQ. I need to target all 3 pods. I have a few options:
- Use static configs with the individual pod DNS names via the headless service, pointing each to `rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local:15692`, `rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local:15692`, and `rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local:15692`

This approach works with native Kubernetes resources and doesn't require any CRDs.

Now I need to reload Prometheus after updating the ConfigMap. Since the deployment has `--web.enable-lifecycle` enabled, I can trigger a reload by hitting the `/-/reload` endpoint rather than restarting the pod.

content.text: The Prometheus config only scrapes `localhost:9090` — it has no RabbitMQ job. The RabbitMQ metrics endpoint is confirmed reachable on port 15692. I'll patch the ConfigMap to add all 3 pod targets via the headless service DNS names, then reload Prometheus.

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a"

kubectl -n $NS patch configmap prometheus-config --type merge -p '{
  "data": {
    "prometheus.yml": "global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: "prometheus"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["localhost:9090"]
  - job_name: "rabbitmq"
    metrics_path: /metrics
    static_configs:
      - targets:
          - "rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
          - "rabbitmq-1.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
          - "rabbitmq-2.rabbitmq-headless.karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a.svc.cluster.local:15692"
"
  }
}'
input.description: Patch Prometheus ConfigMap to add RabbitMQ scrape job targeting all 3 pods via headless service

content.text: Now trigger a live reload of Prometheus (it was started with `--web.enable-lifecycle`):

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a"
# Reload Prometheus config via the lifecycle endpoint
kubectl -n $NS exec curl-test -- curl -s -X POST http://prometheus.${NS}.svc.cluster.local:8000/-/reload
echo "exit: $?"
input.description: Trigger Prometheus config reload via lifecycle API

content.thinking: The reload was successful (empty body, exit 0 is fine for the reload endpoint). Now let me wait a few seconds for Prometheus to scrape the targets and then check the targets status.

content.text: Good — empty body + exit 0 means the reload was accepted. Let me wait for Prometheus to scrape and then verify:

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a"
sleep 20
kubectl -n $NS exec curl-test -- curl -s "http://prometheus.${NS}.svc.cluster.local:8000/api/v1/targets" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
for t in data['data']['activeTargets']:
    print(t['labels'].get('job'), t['labels'].get('instance'), t['health'], t.get('lastError',''))
"

content.thinking:
The reload didn't pick up the new config, or there's an issue. Only `localhost:9090` is showing. The Prometheus instance may not have re-read the config from the ConfigMap since the ConfigMap volume isn't automatically synced immediately.

Let me check what the actual config file looks like inside the running Prometheus pod, then check when ConfigMap volumes are refreshed.

Actually, in Kubernetes, ConfigMap-mounted volumes do get automatically updated, but it can take up to the `kubelet` sync period (typically 60-90 seconds). The `/-/reload` might have been issued before the volume was updated.
I should verify what's actually in the config file on the pod right now.

content.text: The config file may not have propagated to the pod volume yet. Let me check:

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a"
POD=$(kubectl -n $NS get pod -l app=prometheus -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
kubectl -n $NS exec $POD -- cat /etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml
input.description: Check actual config file inside Prometheus pod

# Check what metrics Prometheus has already ingested
kubectl -n $NS exec curl-test -- curl -s "http://prometheus.${NS}.svc.cluster.local:8000/api/v1/label/__name__/values" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
names = [n for n in data['data'] if 'rabbit' in n.lower()]
print('\n'.join(names[:30]))
print(f'Total rabbitmq metrics: {len(names)}')
"
